Rational design of next-generation filovirus vaccines combining glycoprotein stabilization and nanoparticle display with glycan modification
column6: The study demonstrates that self-assembling protein nanoparticles (SApNPs) displaying stabilized filovirus glycoproteins (GPs) from multiple filoviruses, including Bundibugyo virus, significantly enhance immune responses in mice, supporting their potential for broad next-generation filovirus vaccines.
column10:
VACCINE DEVELOPMENT:
This study successfully designed and tested next-generation filovirus vaccines by stabilizing glycoproteins, optimizing nanoparticle delivery, and modifying glycans to enhance immune responses. Key outcomes included stronger antibody production, prolonged lymph node retention, and cross-reactivity against multiple filoviruses, paving the way for broadly effective vaccine development. 

For EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV, comparative analysis of GP?muc-WL2P2 and -WL2P4 constructs revealed that the P4 mutation conferred a more favorable thermostability profile, while trimer yield, purity, and antigenicity remained comparable between P2 and P4.
column11:
This study combined structural biology, protein engineering, and immunology to design filovirus vaccines by stabilizing glycoproteins, optimizing nanoparticle display, and modifying glycans. Techniques included crystal structure analysis, nanoparticle scaffolding, glycan enrichment/trimming, and in vitro/in vivo testing to assess stability, immunogenicity, and antibody responses. 
Here, we sought to obtain atomic details for the more stable EBOV GP?muc-WL2P4.

column2: Multivalent mRNA vaccine platform with compatible antigens conferred broad-spectrum protection against orthoebolaviruses' exposure
column6: In this study, a multivalent broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine ([GPs+NP]@LNP) was developed to defend against EBOV, BDBV and SUDV strains. In mouse models, the coordinated antigenic interaction of nucleoproteins and glycoproteins highlights the potential of the vaccine as a promising candidate to protect against all three major pathogenic orthoebolaviruses.
column7: Vaccination immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Vaccine development
column10:
VACCINE IMMUNITY: [GPs+NP]@LNP demonstrated superior immunogenicity compared with a simple mixture of monovalent vaccines. [GPs+NP]@LNP conferred complete, broad-spectrum, and long-lasting protection against lethal orthoebolaviruses infection in animal models, with immunity persisting up to 1 y, suggesting strong potential for clinical application.

VACCINE DEVELOPMENT: Developed a broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine, [GPs+NP]@LNP. It delivers a mix of mRNAs encoding glycoproteins (GPs) from all three viruses plus EBOV nucleoprotein (NP), using a single LNP.
column11: Developed and evaluated a broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine, designated [GPs+NP]@LNP, formulated with a single lipid nanoparticle (LNP) platform to encapsulate a mixed mRNA payload encoding the glycoproteins (GPs) of EBOV, BDBV, and SUDV, along with the nucleoprotein (NP) of EBOV. This rational antigenic combination was designed to engage both humoral and cellular arms of the immune system.

column2: Resilience and residuals beyond containment - the hidden burden of Bundibugyo Ebola virus survivorship sixteen years on: a cross-sectional observational study
column6: Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) survivorship was marked by persistent but heterogeneous multisystemic sequelae, particularly neurological and musculoskeletal symptoms, alongside subtle immunometabolic perturbations and remarkable mental health resilience. These findings support the need for virus-specific post-BVD care and sustained follow-up to inform survivor health policy.
column7: Long term infection characteristics; Mental health
column10:
CLINICAL DATA - Long-term sequelae: 
Survivors exhibited persistent multisystem symptoms, with neurological and musculoskeletal complaints most frequent, headaches (35 %) and visual disturbances (22.5 %). Laboratory findings showed elevated basophils (40 %) and urinary ketones (5 %), indicating possible chronic inflammation and metabolic shifts. Respiratory rates were significantly reduced in survivors (p < 0.001), while other vital signs and biochemical markers were largely within normal ranges. Despite high resilience, 70 % with normal anxiety scores and 62.5 % with normal depression scores, 57.5 % reported persistent stigma.
column11: Cross-sectional observational study involving 40 laboratory-confirmed BVD survivors and 23 age- and sex-matched unexposed community controls. Participants underwent comprehensive clinical evaluations, laboratory testing, and standardised mental health assessments. Statistical comparisons used rank-sum, chi-squared, and correlation analyses.

column2: Computational Design of Broad-Spectrum Ebola Antibodies through Framework and Complementarity-Determining Region Synergistic Optimization
column6: This study describes a computationalexperimental approach for engineering antibodies with stronger and broader activity against diverse Ebola viruses. Modified versions of ADI?15878 and ADI?15946 showed major potency gainsincluding a 7?fold improvement against BDBV and up to 100?fold against Sudan viruswhile retaining stability and cross?virus effectiveness.
column10:
VACCINE DEVELOPMENT:
This study presents a computationalexperimental pipeline designed to improve the potency and breadth of antibodies targeting highly variable Ebola viruses, including Bundibugyo virus (BDBV).  For ADI?15878, a variant called W32G?LC was created through framework grafting and CDR mutagenesis. This engineered antibody showed significantly enhanced neutralization, including a 7?fold improvement against BDBV, while maintaining structural stability. For ADI?15946, docking-guided multi?site light?chain designs produced variants with dramatically improved activityup to 100?fold increases against Sudan viruswithout reducing effectiveness against EBOV or BDBV.
column11: This pipeline employs multiple computational tools to prioritize affinity-enhancing mutations in complementarity-determining regions (CDRs) and to explore indirect effects through framework region (FR) modifications. These include mCSM-AB and mmCSM-AB for mutant screening, Foldseek for FR grafting, and ZDOCK for complex modeling. Candidate antibodies were expressed, purified, and evaluated using pseudovirus neutralization assays, flow cytometry binding assays, and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) affinity measurements.

A highly potent nanobody-based bispecific therapeutic provides broad-spectrum protection against ebolavirus
column6: This study identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that neutralize EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV in vitro and protect female rodents against these pathogens. This positions their bispecific antibody as a strong broad-spectrum anti-ebolavirus candidate.
column10:
THERAPEUTICS: Identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that protect female rodents against EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV. A bispecific antibody (BA2-1A10) via GS linker-mediated IgG-Fc fusion provided highly potent protection against all three viruses in female rodents model and positions it as a strong broad-spectrum anti-ebolavirus candidate.
PROETIN-INHIBITOR/BINDING INTERACTIONS: Identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that neutralize EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV in vitro. 1A10 and BA2 bind conserved but non-overlapping epitopes near the GP1 base and GP2s internal fusion loop (IFL), and biochemical analyses revealed their distinct neutralization mechanisms.
column11: Researchers identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that neutralize Ebola virus, Sudan virus, and Bundibugyo virus in vitro and provide protection in rodent models, then used cryo-EM and biochemical analyses to determine how each antibody binds distinct conserved epitopes on the viral glycoprotein. Based on these structural insights, they engineered a bispecific antibody (BA2-1A10) using IgG-Fc fusion, and tested its protective efficacy in female rodents as a broad-spectrum therapeutic candidate.

column2: Engineered Bispecific Antibodies Achieve Broad and Potent Protection Against Multiple Ebolavirus Species
column6: Using both recombinant vesicular stomatitis virus expressing glycoproteins from Ebola virus, Sudan virus, and Bundibugyo virus (rVSV-EBOV/SUDV/BDBV) infection models and authentic mouse-adapted EBOV challenge systems, we demonstrate that these bispecific constructs not only retained the parental neutralization potency but also exhibited markedly enhanced cross-species protection.
column10:
VACCINE DEVELOPMENT:
Engineered bispecific antibodies, particularly the IgG?ScFv construct S1+5, markedly enhanced neutralization potency against Bundibugyo virus compared with the parental monoclonal antibodies. While one parental antibody showed almost no BDBV activity, the bispecific designs restored strong binding and reduced PRNT?? values to the low?nanogram range. In  mouse model, S1+5 provided 100% protection against lethal BDBV infection and prevented virus?induced tissue damage. Additionally, S1+5 showed a high barrier to viral escape, maintaining activity across serial BDBV passaging where other antibodies lost effectiveness..
column11: In this study, we engineered three bsAb formats-CrossMabÛ, DVD-IgG, and IgG-ScFv-directed against distinct ebolavirus epitopes and systematically characterized their antiviral activities. Evaluated binding and neutralization against EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV using ELISA, recombinant VSV pseudovirus assays, and authentic virus plaque?reduction neutralization tests. Finally, they assessed viral escape through serial passaging under antibody pressure and tested in vivo protection in IFNAR?/? mice challenged with rVSV?EBOV, rVSV?SUDV, or rVSV?BDBV.

column2: Nanobody targeting glycan cap confers broad orthoebolavirus neutralization
column6: Identified a high-affinity nanobody, Bundibugyo virus (BDBV)-Nb02 that neutralizes both EBOV and BDBV with high potency and nanomolar affinity and further bivalent engineering enhances neutralization potency by up to 56-fold. Residues F248 and NP278/279 are critical for broad neutralization of Ebolaviruses.
column10: PROTEIN INHIBITOR/BINDING INTERACTIONS: Researchers identified a high-affinity nanobody (BDBV-Nb02) from a synthetic phage display library that targets the glycan cap of Bundibugyo virus glycoprotein and potently neutralizes both Bundibugyo and Ebola virus pseudoviruses. Engineering the nanobody into a bivalent format greatly improved neutralization potency by up to 56-fold, while structural analyses showed it targets a conserved glycan cleft, though Fc-fusion reduced its effectiveness by disrupting access to key epitopes.
column11: Screened a fully synthetic phage display library and identified a nanobody, BDBV-Nb02, which targets the glycan cap region of BDBV GP and characterized its binding kinetics and neutralization activity against EBOV and BDBV pseudoviruses, and demonstrated that a bivalent format markedly enhances its neutralizing potency. Using epitope competition assays, site-directed mutagenesis, and molecular docking analyses, further defined key residues involved in neutralization and elucidated the structural basis underlying its cross-reactive activity.

column10: MUTATIONS:Identified a specific k-mer anchor sequence (notably CGCG) that appears exclusively in Bundibugyo (BDBV) genomes, suggesting it may represent a mutation pattern unique to this virus type. column11: The study developed a computational k-merbased feature extraction algorithm that identifies biologically meaningful anchor sequences by analyzing reverse, complementary, and reverse-complementary k-mers to model mutation patterns across viral genomes, including Bundibugyo virus. It then quantified these anchors using a weighted interval entropy measure and evaluated the method.

column6: This rapid review and meta-analysis aimed to quantify the effect of diagnostic and response delays on outbreak-level mortality for Ebola virus disease (EBOD) in Uganda (2000-2022).  By species, Sudan ebolavirus outbreaks (n?=?5) had a CFR of 44.6% (95% CI: 33.7%55.6%), Bundibugyo ebolavirus (n?=?1) 24.8% (95% CI: 18.2%32.1%), and Zaire ebolavirus (n?=?1) 100% (95% CI: 61.2%100.0%).

RISK FACTORS- MORTALITY:
Bundibugyo ebolavirus patients and found that haemorrhagic manifestations were significantly more common in those who died (Fishers exact p = 0.05) and that each additional clinically observed symptom increased the odds of death by ~ 31% (OR 1.31; 95% CI 1.041.82) .
column11:
We searched PubMed, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, WHO Global Index Medicus, and grey literature through 30 April 2025. Eligible reports described laboratory-confirmed human EBOD in Uganda (20002022) and reported case counts, deaths, or quantitative timeliness metrics. Outbreak-level CFRs were meta-analyzed using random-effects models with FreemanTukey transformation (metafor package in R). Mixed-effects meta-regression assessed the association between continuous delay metrics and transformed CFR.

 We synthesized data from 15 studies covering seven laboratory-confirmed Ebola outbreaks in Uganda (20002022) (Table 2), comprising four Sudan ebolavirus waves (Gulu 2000; Luwero 2011; Kibaale 2012; Luwero hospital 2012), one Bundibugyo ebolavirus wave (Bundibugyo 2007), and two Zaire ebolavirus importations (Kasese 2019 and 2020).

column2: Rapid risk assessment: Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda
column6:
The overall risk level to the Canadian population of acquiring Bundibugyo virus from the ongoing outbreak in the DRC and Uganda is currently low (moderate uncertainty). 

The likelihood of importation of Bundibugyo virus into Canada in the next two weeks is low (moderate uncertainty), given the low travel volume between currently affected regions and Canada, and existing travel advisories that recommend against all travel to the outbreak-affected areas in the DRC due to ongoing safety and security concerns. The impact on an affected individual is estimated to be severe (low uncertainty), given the serious clinical manifestations of BVD, its high case fatality rate and lack of approved vaccines or antivirals.

If importation into Canada were to occur, the impact of BVD on the general population in Canada would be minor (low uncertainty), due to the limited number of secondary cases anticipated among close contacts, strong diagnostic and health system capacity, and existing protocols for outbreak response, case and contact management and infection prevention and control.

column10:
* only key studies in humans are extracted below. Details available for human, non-human primates and animal models: 

CLINICAL DATA- ACUTE- SEQUELAE:
Survivors of BDBV exhibited persistent multisystem symptoms, with neurological and musculoskeletal complaints most frequent, headaches (35 %) and visual disturbances (22.5 %). Laboratory findings showed elevated basophils (40 %) and urinary ketones (5 %), indicating possible chronic inflammation and metabolic shifts. Respiratory rates were significantly reduced in survivors (p < 0.001), while other vital signs and biochemical markers were largely within normal ranges. Despite high resilience, 70 % with normal anxiety scores and 62.5 % with normal depression scores, 57.5 % reported persistent stigma.

CLINICAL DATA - LONG-TERM SEQUELAE:
Survivors of the Bundibugyo Ebola virus were at significantly increased risk of ocular deficits (retro-orbital pain [RR 4ñ3, 95% CI 1ñ9-9ñ6; p<0ñ0001], blurred vision [1ñ9, 1ñ1-3ñ2; p=0ñ018]), hearing loss (2ñ3, 1ñ2-4ñ5; p=0ñ010), difficulty swallowing (2ñ1, 1ñ1-3ñ9; p=0ñ017), difficulty sleeping (1ñ9, 1ñ3-2ñ8; p=0ñ001), arthralgias (2ñ0, 1ñ1-3ñ6; p=0ñ020), and various constitutional symptoms controlling for age and sex. Chronic health problems (prevalence ratio [PR] 2ñ1, 95% CI 1ñ2-3ñ6; p=0ñ008) and limitations due to memory loss or confusion (PR 5ñ8, 1ñ5-22ñ4; p=0ñ010) were also reported more frequently by survivors of Bundibugyo Ebola virus.

RISK FACTORS- MORTALITY:
Correlation between fatal infection and immune response in BDBV in 44 patient sample found that fatal infection was associated with high levels of viral antigen, low levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as IL-1?, IL-1?, IL-6, TNF-?, and high levels of immunosuppressor cytokines like IL-10. 

IMMUNITY:
Neutralization of antibodies and immune response is categorized and mapped for both previous infection and vaccination from Ebola virus vaccine. 

DIAGNOSTIC TEST ACCRUARY:
Evidence gap map breaks out the different assays used for infection and outcome. Specifically, ELISA, Microneutralization, FRNT/PRNT, Live virus, pseudovirus, Other neutralising assay, ELISPOT, Intracellular cytokine staining, other Tcell assays.

An Evidence (gap) Map is a systematic evidence curation tool that displays all the available evidence relevant to a specific research question or topic of interest as a matrix. Bundibugyo virus map 18 May 2026 and end date search 29 May 2026 with consistent updates. 

Questions: What tools and evidence exist, and where are the evidence gaps, in measures of immune response and immunity for MARV, SUDV and EBOV?*  What is an appropriate typology/framework for comprehensive classification of tools and evidence in preclinical vaccine assessment?


The study will address this question by identifying and organising the evidence across the following domains for all pathogens:

-Immunoassay landscape 
-Immune responses and immune protection following infection and vaccination 
-Animal models developed for use in testing vaccine and mAbs candidates 
-Safety, immunogenicity and efficacy data from animal challenge studies
-Clinical safety and immunogenicity data following administration of vaccine and mAbs candidates


PubMed, Embase, Europe PMC.

For Mpox, Lassa and Bundibugyo PubMed and Embase were searched. Europe PMC was not searched in the living update as later pathogens as the initial maps indicated Embase now extensively covers all the major preprint servers

VACCINE IMMUNITY:
Detectable cross-reactive antibody responses against BDBV were observed across vaccine groups, timepoints, and age categories. column6: Researchers assembled a half-century, outbreak-level record of human orthoebolavirus events from public CDC and WHO sources and study the determinants of the case-fatality rate (CFR) with interpretable, low-variance ensemble learning under leave-one-out validation. Viral species is the dominant driver of pooled CFR near 70% for Zaire, 51% for Sudan, 24% for Bundibugyo, and outbreak scale is the second strongest feature, while contextual covariates carry a secondary signal.
column10: Researchers assembled a half-century, outbreak-level record of human orthoebolavirus events from public CDC and WHO sources and study the determinants of the case-fatality rate (CFR) with interpretable, low-variance ensemble learning under leave-one-out validation. Viral species is the dominant driver of pooled CFR near 70% for Zaire, 51% for Sudan, 24% for Bundibugyo, and outbreak scale is the second strongest feature, while contextual covariates carry a secondary signal. Read against the 2026 Bundibugyo emergency, the results locate the current outbreak at the low-lethality end of the species spectrum while underlining that lower lethality, late species confirmation and the absence of a species-matched vaccine combine into a configuration in which scale, rather than per-case fatality, is the quantity to watch.
column11: The study uses interpretable ensemble learning (gradient boosting, random forest, elastic-net regression) with leave-one-out cross-validation to analyze Ebola outbreak CFRs from 19762026. The analysis focuses on outbreak-level data from CDC and WHO, emphasizing methodological transparency and generalizability.

column6: This rapid review highlights the genomic and molecular characteristics of BDBV, focusing on mutations identified in recent and past outbreaks; comparative genomic analyses based on current available genomic sequences indicate that the 2026 outbreak likely originated from a novel zoonotic spillover event rather than direct continuation of the 2007 Uganda or 2012 DRC outbreaks. column10: MUTATIONS/PHYLOGENETIC ANALYSIS:  Genomic analyses suggest this outbreak stems from a novel zoonotic spillover, distinct from the 2007 and 2012 outbreaks, with shared and unique mutations identified in the glycoprotein (GP) gene and other viral regions. column6:
Analysis of 10 BDBV sequences from DRC and Uganda share a most recent common ancestor between March 1 and March 15, 2026, and exhibit significant genetic diversity, indicating the outbreak had been circulating undetected for several weeks. Preliminary estimates suggest an epidemic doubling time (range: 15.2 to 24.5 days) and a reproduction number (??0) above 1 (range: 1.31 to 1.55). These findings should be interpreted cautiously due to substantial uncertainty. 
Estimates of doublings per year was determined using a generation time estimate from incubation and infectious period estimates from previous outbreak.
column7: Reproduction Number; Size or Duration estimate; Pathogen mutations; Phylogenetic analysis/molecular epidemiology
column10:
REPRODUCTION NUMBER:
The corresponding reproduction number (??0) estimates are centred above 1, with mean estimates ranging from 1.31 to 1.55, although substantial uncertainty remains because of the limited number of sequences currently available.  The reproduction number was estimated from the number of doublings per year using a generation time estimate derived from previous outbreak published incubation and infectious-period estimates for Bundibugyo virus disease.

OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION - DOUBLING TIME:
The estimated epidemic doubling time is relatively slow but highly uncertain. Across the six substitution-rate assumptions, the mean estimated doubling time ranges from 15.2 to 24.5 days,  although the corresponding uncertainty intervals are wide and include zero and should therefore be interpreted cautiously.

MUTATIONS & PHYLOGENETIC ANALYSIS: The sampled viruses appear to share a most recent common ancestor in early-to-mid March 2026 and are already genetically diverse. Researchers identified 23 unique mutations across the 10 sequences sampled.
column11:
Outbreak-associated BDBV sequences were downloaded from Pathoplexus and were aligned to the reference genome FJ217161.1 using MAFFT v7.490, then manually inspected in AliView. Ancestral node reconstruction was performed using IQ-TREE v3.0.1 and the best-fitting substitution model, HKY+F+I. This analysis was used to reconstruct the consensus sequence of the most recent common ancestor (MRCA) of the sampled outbreak sequences. This reconstructed MRCA sequence was then used as the root for the outbreak mutation reconstruction.

We used systematically collected Ebola virus disease parameters7 available through {epireview}8 to identify previous estimates of epidemiological delays for Bundibugyo virus disease.9,10 This gave a generation time estimate of 9.6 days. The uncertainty intervals for ??0 were obtained by propagating the 95% HPD interval from the estimated number of doublings per year.

The estimated growth rates and ??0 values are likely affected by sampling bias. The sequences included here were collected between 03 May 2026 and 20 May 2026, and therefore represent a short time window around the identification and declaration of the BVD outbreak.11 Situation reports12,13 and independent epidemiological analyses14 suggest that, even at the time these sequences were generated, the outbreak was already substantially larger than the available sequence data alone would indicate.

All analyses were performed in Delphy1 and run until the minimum effective sample size was greater than 100. The reproduction number was estimated from the number of doublings per year using a generation time estimate derived from published incubation and infectious-period estimates for Bundibugyo virus disease.7-10

column11: CDC used a branching process model to simulate BVD outbreaks where each simulated outbreak was initialized with one infected person, who represented the person first infected from a zoonotic source (a spillover event). This person infected a randomly generated number of additional persons based on assumptions about the basic reproductive number ([R0]. Any infected persons were added to the simulation at times selected according to the distribution of intervals from one infection to the next, causing further infection and this simulation process continued until either 1) none of the infected persons in a generation caused any secondary infections, indicating termination of the outbreak or 2) the simulation reached 5,000 deaths, indicating a very large and exponentially growing outbreak.

column6:
The authors found that traveler screening via symptom
screening or molecular testing will miss the majority of Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) infected travelers, and should be complemented by post-arrival quarantine and monitoring of sufficient duration to detect those with long incubation periods. These findings underscore the limitations of border screening and the importance of complementary measures like post-arrival quarantine to prevent local importation of BVD.
column10:
Public Health Measures - Border measures
A model to predict the proportion of infected travelers missed by symptom screening at arrival and post-arrival quarantine with symptom monitoring was developed. The model estimates that arrival symptom screening will miss 67.6%86.3% of infected travelers in a steady state outbreak within the source country.  If all arriving travelers are subject to a universal post-arrival quarantine and monitoring, the researchers estimate that a 7day quarantine will miss 26.3% of infections. If both interventions are applied, the combination of arrival screening with post-arrival universal quarantine is predicted to miss 17.8%22.7% of infected travelers.

Public Health Measures - Quarantine/isolation
Researchers conclude that travel screening is beneficial however, quarantining is more effective in detecting  infected travellers. Increasing quarantine duration decreases the proportion of infections missed, but even a 21-day quarantine is predicted to be imperfect if the incubation period distribution is heavy-tailed. Empirical data to better characterize the shape of this distribution would greatly improve predictions and therefore, quarantine decisions must weigh the increasing cost of longer quarantine periods against the diminishing benefit of reduced risk of evasion.
column11: Researchers  developed a simple model to predict the proportion of infected travelers missed by symptom screening at arrival and post-arrival quarantine with symptom monitoring. This model analyzes the effectiveness of symptom-based screening and quarantine measures in detecting infected travelers, considering variables like incubation period, symptom onset, and epidemiological dynamics. It derives failure rates for screening and quarantine programs under different scenarios, including steady-state and exponentially growing outbreaks, and provides estimates for the Bundibugyo virus incubation period

column10: EPI PARAMETER- Cluster investigation: BEACON detected four temporally proximal VHF-compatible illness signals: (1) March 9, North Kivu Provincesuspected Ebola case under investigation with unresolved laboratory results; (2) March 10, Kasaÿ Provincefatal hemorrhagic illness with secondary cases and negative Ebola PCR; (3) March 30, Burundi35-case undiagnosed cluster near the DRC border with 5 deaths, negative testing for major filoviruses and >200 pathogens, pending metagenomic sequencing; (4) April 22, South Sudanthree suspected VHF cases with negative initial testing. All four signals shared a similar diagnostic phenotype: VHF-compatible presentation, mobilization of investigation teams, negative initial testing, and no publicly reported confirmed etiology. None were formally reported to have been resolved. The detection of four unresolved VHF signals preceding the confirmed BDBV outbreak highlights gaps in formal follow-up mechanisms for negative cases and fragmented regional diagnostic coordination.

Subsequently, they modeled the RdRp BDBV 2026 protein and docked RTP with the RdRp catalyist domain. RTP is an approved antiviral drug for SARS-CoV-2. The binding affinity of RTP to BDBV 2026 RdRp was found similar to the binding affinity of RTP to SARS-CoV-2 RdRp catalytic domain. RTP was found to tightly fit within the pocket site and the complex of the RTP and RdRp showed dense molecular interactions aiding in stabilizing the complex.

They sequenced a clinical isolate (CL023114) of a 59-year-old BDBV infected patient which was identified as travel case. Genome sequencing was performed using the Illumina NextSeq and the sequence was submitted to the Pathoplexus platform with accession number of PP_006XCJJ.1. To predict an accurate three dimensional (3D) structural model of the L protein (RdRp), the Google DeepMind-based AlphaFold3 algorithm was employed.

column2: Mapping the Global Evidence Base of Bundibugyo Ebolavirus Disease: A Systematic Scoping Review of Research Gaps and Preparedness Priorities
column6: Only 23% of included studies specifically examined Bundibugyo virus disease (BDBV), with most research focused on other ebolaviruses, particularly Zaire ebolavirus, and concentrated on epidemiological drivers and clinical features. Major gaps remain in response-related research, including the absence of licensed BDBV-specific vaccines, therapeutics, and validated rapid diagnostics, while survivor outcomes are poorly characterized and study quality is limited by moderate risk of bias from missing outcome data and selective reporting.

column10: Only 23% of the included studies specifically addressed BDBV, with the remainder focusing on broader ebolavirus topics dominated by Zaire ebolavirus (EBOV). Applying the conceptual model revealed pronounced asymmetries across the research domains: the literature remains heavily concentrated within upstream epidemiological drivers and clinical features. Conversely, critical health system response domains lack validated tools; no licensed vaccine, specific therapeutic agent, or point-of-care rapid diagnostic test validated for BDBV currently exists. While cross-reactive monoclonal antibodies show preclinical efficacy, clinical-grade validation remains absent, and longitudinal survivor outcomes are sparsely documented. Systemic moderators, particularly weak health infrastructure and conflict, continue to compound these gaps. Methodological risk of bias was moderate, driven by missing outcome data (42% of studies) and selective reporting (41%). Conclusions BDBV remains understudied relative to its epidemic potential.
column11: This systematic scoping review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses Extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. The protocol was prospectively registered on the Open Science Framework (available at: https://osf.io/q8r63). Searches were executed programmatically using a custom Python 3.12 script on 1 May 2026 across PubMed/MEDLINE, OpenAlex, and Semantic Scholar, supplemented by manual searches of Scopus, Web of Science, WHO Disease Outbreak News, Africa CDC, medRxiv, and ReliefWeb. The PubMed strategy combined MeSH terms and free-text synonyms across 24 topic-specific queries.

column6: This guidance includes information about incidents and outbreaks of Ebola and Marburg, both viral haemorrhagic fevers (VHF). As of June 10, 2026, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Uganda are experiencing a public health emergency of international concern due to a new outbreak of Ebola disease caused by the Bundibugyo virus, with 598 confirmed cases and 101 deaths reported in the DRC, and 19 confirmed cases and 2 deaths reported in Uganda. This outbreak is part of a series of Ebola and Marburg virus disease incidents and outbreaks that have occurred globally from 2016 to 2026, with various countries including Ethiopia, Rwanda, Tanzania, and Ghana being affected, and the DRC being the most frequently impacted country.

column11: To produce early estimates of the epidemic size in the Democratic Republic of the Congo as of May 27, 2026, The authors conducted scenario based analyses using two simple independent methods, with the scarce information available and making plausible assumptions about the surveillance, severity, and growth of the outbreak. The first method uses an analytical framework linking reported suspected and confirmed deaths, of which the authors assume a proportion is due to Ebola, with estimates of the case fatality ratio (33% [95% CI 2640]; defined as the proportion of patients who die as a result of a Bundibugyo virus infection) and the time from symptom onset to death (gamma distribution mean 11ñ37 days [SD 5ñ41]) derived from previous Bundibugyo virus. The second method combined information on the three confirmed imported Bundibugyo virus disease cases reported in Uganda (as of May 27, 2026), estimates of daily international travel volumes from Ituri and Nord Kivu provinces to Uganda, assumptions about the epidemic doubling time, and we presumed that all cases in symptomatic travellers will be detected at the time of recovery or death.

column6: ECDC recommends temporary deferral of asymptomatic individuals donating substances of human origin (SoHO) for at least six weeks after arriving from areas with Bundibugyo virus community transmission and in the context of the current Ebola disease outbreak, individuals who are being monitored due to contact with a patient with an infection, or other exposure to Bundibugyo virus are ineligible to donate SoHO for at least six weeks from the beginning of the monitoring period. ECDC recommends a permanent deferral from donation of blood, cells and tissues for donors who have recovered from Ebola disease. ECDC also recommends that individuals who have had sexual contact with persons who have recovered from Ebola disease should be deferred from donating SoHO for at least six weeks after exposure, irrespective of the time elapsed since the recovery of the convalescent sexual contact.

column6: This is a case report of the Uganda 2026 BVD index case: a male patient who traveled from the Democratic Republic of the Congo to Uganda and was admitted to a private hospital in Kampala on May 11 and died on May 14. The 2026 BDBV genome formed a distinct lineage approximately equidistant from the 2007-2008 Butalya and 2012 Isiro variants, differing by 216-227 nucleotides (~1.2% sequence divergence).

column10:
Clinical Data - The patients clinical history revealed a complex cross-border pattern of healthcare-seeking behavior: he reportedly sought medical care in the DRC and later at a private clinic in Hoima District, western Uganda, where no blood work was performed before he left, and then self-referred to a private hospital in Kampala. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit on 14 May with sepsis and multiorgan dysfunction, acute fulminant hepatitis, acute kidney injury, atrial flutter with a rapid ventricular response, and possible disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). He developed sudden cardiorespiratory arrest, and death was declared after unsuccessful  cardiopulmonary resuscitation. 

Phylogenetic analysis - Phylogenetic analysis showed that the 2026 Uganda genome forms a distinct lineage relative to both the 20072008 Uganda and 2012 DRC BDBV genomes. Pairwise genome comparisons  identified 219 nucleotide differences between the 2026 genome and the 20072008 Uganda genomes, and 216227 nucleotide differences relative to the 2012 DRC genomes, corresponding 158 to approximately 1.2% sequence divergence across comparable nucleotide sites.

Intriguingly, also vaccination with YF- EBO or YF-SUD protected mice from such BDBV surrogate challenge; while YF-BDB and YF-EBO fully protected against respective heterologous EBOV or BDBV infection, they conferred only partial protection against lethal challenge in a comparable Sudan virus (SUDV) model.

A yellow fever 17D-vectored BDBV vaccine (YF-BDB) expressing the BDBV glycoprotein (GP) as antigen conferred homologous protection in Ifnar?/? mice, preventing systemic viral dissemination and providing survival following surrogate BDBV challenge. Intriguingly, also vaccination with YF- EBO or YF-SUD protected mice from such BDBV surrogate challenge. Conversely, while YF-BDB and YF-EBO fully protected against respective heterologous EBOV or BDBV infection, they  conferred only partial protection against lethal challenge in a comparable SUDV model. Serological analyses revealed comparable titers of cross-reactive antibodies across all vaccine groups. However, neutralization, especially cross-neutralization, was limited suggesting a major role for non-neutralizing mechanisms in protection.

column2: Estimating the effectiveness of syndromic screening at airports for Bundibugyo ebolavirus disease
column6: For a 12-hour international flight from DRC or Uganda at 86% screening sensitivity, the authors estimated 65% of infected travellers would arrive undetected (95% CrI: 3876%). The main driver of this outcome is the relative duration of the the incubation period (approximately 7.7 days) and the onset-to-severe-disease interval (approximately 4 days): most infected travellers board before symptom onset and are undetectable by any syndromic screen, whilst those who are symptomatic progress rapidly to illness severe enough to preclude travel.
column10: Public Health Measures - Border measures: At 86% sensitivity on a 12-hour international flight, combined syndromic exit and entry screening will fail to detect approximately 65% of infected travellers (95% CrI: 3876%). The relatively short onset-to-severe-disease interval (mean approximately four days) means that symptomatic travellers progress to detectable illness quickly, yet the incubation period (approximately 7.7 days) means that most infected travellers will board before symptom onset. Exit syndromic screening sensitivity has a larger marginal effect on the undetected fraction than entry screening sensitivity, because it acts on a larger at-risk window: any traveller already symptomatic at departure can be caught at exit, whereas entry screening only adds value for those who become symptomatic during the flight or who were missed due to imperfect screening at exit. When exit screening is absent entirely (sensitivity 0%) but entry screening is maintained at 86%, 67% of travellers remain undetected, compared with 73% when entry screening is absent and exit is maintained at 86%. This asymmetry reflects the longer opportunity for symptom detection that the pre-flight period provides relative to the flight itself.
column11: Authors used a stochastic individual-based simulation model to evaluate the probability that a Bundibugyo ebolavirus disease (BVD) infected traveller would be detected at (i) exit screening before departure, (ii) as severely ill during the flight, (iii) at entry screening on arrival, or (iv) remain undetected throughout, using natural-history parameters from a Bayesian re-analysis of the 2012 Isiro outbreak.  Natural-history parameters (incubation period, onset-to-severe-disease delay) are derived from the 2007 Uganda and 2012 DRC BVD outbreaks. The epidemic doubling time range used in the growth-phase sensitivity analysis is estimated from the current outbreak.

column2: Estimation of the importation risk of Bundibugyo virus into the EU/EEA in June 2026
column6: Based on mathematical modelling, the authors estimate approximately one importation of  Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) per 24 000 travellers (90% Uncertainty Interval, UI: 13 00054 000) from the main outbreak region (North Kivu and Ituri, DRC) to the EU/EEA.  The authors also estimate the probability of at least one BDBV importation into the EU/EEA from 1125 June 2026 to be 0.45% (90% UI: 0.20%0.85%), under the hypothetical assumption that 100 people travel from the outbreak region to the EU/EEA during this period.
column10: Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration: While sporadic BDBV importations into the EU/EEA cannot be ruled out, mathematical modelling suggests that the probability of importation from 11 to 25 June is very low. These results apply to importation of BDBV from the general population of Ituri and North Kivu. Humanitarian aid workers or healthcare care personnel returning from the outbreak region to the EU/EEA, who the authors assume would be medically evacuated from the affected areas with application of appropriate infection prevention and control measures, need to be considered separately. As one BDBV importation is expected per 24 000 travellers from the outbreak region, the vast majority of travellers will not be infected. However, since early symptoms of BDBV infections overlap with many other conditions, a potentially large number of travellers will show similar symptoms as BDBV infections without being infected with BDBV (i.e. false positives).
column11: The authors used a two-step approach to estimate the risk of importation of BDBV into the EU/EEA. First, they developed a compartmental model for the BDBV transmission. The model concentrates in the Ituri and North Kivu provinces in DRC, where most BDBV infections have been reported from. The study period was defined as 1125 June 2026. The estimated population size of the outbreak region is ?? = 13,392,200. The mathematical model in the first step partitions the population of the outbreak region into six compartments or disease states: those not infected (susceptible); those who are exposed, which we defined as those who are infected but not yet infectious (latently infected); those who are infectious and will recover; those who are infectious and will die; those who recovered; and the deceased. In the second step, the authors build on the output of the mathematical model to estimate the probability of at least one BDBV importation into the EU/EEA.

column6: The table and accompanying algorithm provide guidance for EU/EEA public health authorities, decision-makers, and healthcare professionals on risk categorisation and management of individuals potentially exposed to Ebola disease. The guidance applies to contacts of confirmed or probable cases following travel to, residence in, or work in Ebola outbreak-affected areas, as well as to occupational exposures. As long as an outbreak is ongoing, individuals arriving from affected regions may develop Ebola disease after entering non-affected countries, therefore, minimising transmission relies on rapid case detection and isolation, effective contact tracing, and strict infection prevention and control (IPC) measures.

Its purpose is to support timely clinical testing needed for patient assessment and care, while minimizing risk to laboratory personnel and limiting unnecessary disruptions to laboratory operations. This guidance is intended to inform local risk assessments and operational decisions in diagnostic clinical laboratory settings.

column2: Overview of available modelling evidence to inform the scale and potential spread of Bundibugyo virus in the current Ebola disease outbreak
column6: This overview assessed the modelling evidence to inform scale and spread of BDBV in DRC and Uganda. Multiple modelling groups suggest that the true size of the outbreak is larger than reported with one model estimating that cumulative infections as of 13 June were between 3.0 and 10.2 times the reported number of cases (90% credible interval). Current modelling estimates are highly uncertain due to data limitations. Relative risk of importation is highest in Rwanda, Tanzania and Kenya and low risk risk in EU/EEA.
column7: Reproduction Number; Size or Duration estimate; Mortality
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION:
The MRC Centre for Global Infectious Disease Analysis at Imperial College London estimated that, as of 16 May 2026, the true number of infections was between 1.2 and 2.4 times the reported case count. The Epistorm group, led by Northeastern University in the United States (US) and supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (US CDC), estimated the size of the outbreak around the same date to be 1.16 (90% credible interval (CrI): 0.42.7) times larger than reported. Epiforecasts, a consortium that includes modellers from the London School of Health and Tropical Medicine, has been continuously updating its estimates of the outbreak size. On 13 June, they estimated that the true number of infections was between 3.0 and 10.2 times higher than reported (90% CrI).


Relative risk of importation (i.e. the share of risk allocated to a location) is higher for the countries with the highest connectivity to the outbreak areas, with Rwanda, Tanzania and Kenya identified as the countries with the highest relative risk. Estimates of absolute risk of importation (i.e. the probability of risk to a particular country or locality) into the EU/EEA under current prevalence levels and assuming historical travel patterns suggest only a remote chance of importation in the next two-week period. European countries with the highest connectivity to the current outbreak areas are at higher relative risk of importation; these include the UK, Belgium, Germany France and Italy.   Mid-term scenario projections from US CDC suggest a 65% probability of over 20 000 infections over the next three months under the assumption that 20% of patients are effectively isolated and no other control measures are implemented. 

MORTALITY:
Epiforecasts conducted a model fitted to multiple surveillance data streams (suspected and confirmed cases and deaths in DRC, and exported cases and deaths in Uganda) estimated a CFR 0.28 to 0.61 (90% CrI).

The WHO reported that between May 2026 to present (DRC and Uganda)  CFR is 17.4% (93 deaths of 534 cases). Other historical outbreaks reported CFR ranging from 0-88% depending on the country and outbreak.

REPRODUCTION NUMBER:
Epiforecasts conducted a model fitted to multiple surveillance data streams (suspected and confirmed cases and deaths in DRC, and exported cases and deaths in Uganda) estimated a reproduction number (R0) of 1.29 to 3.06 (90%CrI).
column11:
ECDC experts conducted a pragmatic assessment of the modelling evidence available at the time of writing. Given the rapidly evolving nature of the outbreak and the need to support situational awareness, a formal systematic or rapid review methodology was not used. The assessment drew on the authors participation in international modelling networks and communities of practice, discussions with subject-matter experts, engagement with modelling groups actively working on the outbreak, and monitoring of publicly available reports, preprints, publications and technical outputs relevant to the ongoing outbreak.


At this early stage of the outbreak, modelling is constrained by limited data and considerable uncertainty around key epidemiological parameters. Consequently, analyses rely on assumptions and parameter estimates derived from previous outbreaks, which may not fully reflect the current situation. Multiple combinations of parameter values can plausibly explain the available observations, meaning that a wide range of model projections may be consistent with the data. Such variation primarily reflects uncertainty about the outbreak itself rather than differences in model quality. In addition, modelling assumptions, parameter choices and the interpretation of results may be influenced by analysts prior expectations, methodological preferences or the framing of the questions being addressed.

column2: Preparedness and response for imported cases of Ebola disease into an EU/EEA country
column6: This report presents an overview of health preparedness and response planning elements for the potential importation of a case of Ebola disease into a European Union/European Economic Area (EU/EEA) Member State health system. This document is organised into four focus areas, representing the potential health system contact points of an imported Ebola disease case. All four areas must operate effectively and in coordination with public health services to prevent further community transmission.
column10:
Infection Prevention and Control - General workflow guidance: The four focus areas highlighted in this document include: Potential health system first contact for an unknown case of Ebola disease,  In-country transport of a probable or confirmed case of Ebola disease, Designated treatment facility for Ebola disease case(s), Medical evacuation of an Ebola disease case and Overarching public health planning elements.  For each focus area, a checklist is presented as a stand-alone table, subdivided into the organisational capabilities required to: prepare for the possibility of an imported Ebola disease case: Planning, protocols and training'; recognise individuals as Persons Under Investigation (PUI) or probable cases of Ebola disease: Case Recognition; manage PUI, probable or confirmed Ebola disease patient effectively, while ensuring the safety of staff who come into contact with them: Patient Management and Staff Protection.    

Public Health Measures - Other: This document also presents an overall preparedness checklist which can serve as a template for public health planning of the response to an imported case of Ebola disease case. This checklist sets out the system elements to plan without outlining the organisational competencies in detail. System elements include: public health preparedness planning, health care infrastructure, and risk communication and community engagement  Infodemic Management (RCCEIM).
column11: This document was developed in the context of the ECDC Public Health Emergency for the Ebola outbreak 2026, drawing  upon multiple ECDC documents on public health preparedness and planning that can assist countries in their overall public health planning for such an event as well as aviation stakeholder guidance.

A Single-Dose Bundibugyo Virus Vaccine Protects Macaques Within 3 Days
column6: Authors evaluated the rapid protection by a single-dose vaccination of vesicular stomatitis virus - Bundibugyo virus (VSV-BDBV) in  cynomolgus macaques (n=5) and showed that vaccination elicited rapid innate and early adaptive immune responses and conferred complete protection from clinical disease against BDBV challenge within 3 days. Vaccinated cynomolgus macaques exhibited minimal clinical signs, limited systemic inflammation, and no infectious virus was isolated from the blood at any time; protection correlated with neutralizing antibodies and Fc effector functionality of the humoral immune response.
column7: Vaccination immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines)
column10:
Vaccine immunity - Protection from infection and other disease outcomes (efficacy/effectiveness): A control group received the VSV-Marburg virus (VSV-MARV) vaccine at 1æ107 PFU by the  intramuscular route 7 days prior to BDBV challenge and resulted in 60% lethality. Viral load quantification showed that the control non-human primates (NHP) developed high titer viremia, specifically the NHPs that succumbed to disease, while the VSV-BDBV-vaccinated NHPs displayed no or low viral RNA levels. No infectious virus was recovered at any time point from the blood of all vaccinated NHPs, BDBV RNA was absent in all swab samples obtained from the vaccinated NHPs

Vaccine immunity - Measures of protective immunity (antibodies/titres/cytokines): The demonstration of rapid onset of protection for species-specific VSV-based vaccines against the human-pathogenic filoviruses indicates that this may be an intrinsic property of the VSV platform dominated early on by the robust innate immune response. The protective efficacy of VSV-based filovirus vaccines altogether, however, is species-specific, driven primarily by the antibody responses directed against the viral GP antigens. Rapid protection appears to be associated with the induction of early antiviral innate immune responses coupled with the accelerated development of functional GP-specific antibodies. Consistent with a benign clinical disease course, vaccinated NHPs exhibited minimal cytokine dysregulation following challenge. Pro-inflammatory cytokines characteristic of severe EBOD, including IL-6, TNF-?, IFN-?, IFN-?, and IFN-?, were markedly reduced compared to the control NHPs.
column11: Fifteen male cynomolgus macaques (Macaca fascicularis) 6-11 years of age and 5.57.9 kg in weight were used for this study and randomly assigned to vaccination groups. Group sizes of n = 5 were determined to be required by power analysis using log-rank test in graph pad/prism under the assumption that 40% of the control animals may survive the infection. To determine a shortened time from vaccination to challenge resulting in uniform protection from disease caused by BDBV infection, cynomolgus macaques (n=5 per group) received a single intramuscular (IM) dose of VSVBDBV (1æ107 PFU) either 7 or 3 days prior to IM challenge with 10,000 median tissue culture infectious dose (TCID50) of BDBV. A control group received the VSV-Marburg virus (VSV-MARV) vaccine at 1æ107 PFU by the IM route 7 days prior to BDBV challenge. The total white blood cell, neutrophil, lymphocyte, and platelet counts were determined from EDTA blood with the IDEXX ProCyte DX analyzer (IDEXX Laboratories, Westbrook, ME). Serum biochemistry including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), glucose, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, and total bilirubin was analyzed on a Vetscan 2 using Preventive care profile disks (Abaxis, Union City, CA).

WHO guidelines for the clinical management of filovirus disease: executive summary
column6: The guidelines address key clinical interventions to improve outcomes for patients with suspected or confirmed filovirus disease. Recommendations cover systematic clinical and laboratory monitoring, oral and intravenous fluid management, assessment and treatment of shock, use of vasopressors, central venous access, antimicrobial therapy, management of haemorrhage and postpartum haemorrhage, and structured follow-up for survivors. Emphasis is placed on high-quality supportive care, individualized clinical decision-making and integration with infection prevention and control measures.

column2: The risk of global Ebola virus spread is low: epidemiology of Ebola disease cases outside Africa, 1976 to May 2026
column3: Primary - article, Observational, Other grey literature report
column6: This study assessed global Ebola transmission by evaluating 28 confirmed Ebola cases outside of Africa (25 primary imported cases and three secondary cases in the US or Europe) of which 27 occurred in 2014-2016 and one case in May 2026. The crude overall risk of exportation was 0.81 cases per 1000 Ebola cases outside of Africa including medically evacuated, latent and secondary cases.  The four latent cases from 300,000 were asymptomatic at screening at exit and entry, suggesting clear guidance on self-monitoring of symptoms for rapid isolation.
column7: Infection risk factors; Size or Duration estimate; Mortality; Hospitalizations; Other clinical data; Border measure - PHM
column8: Human - General population, Human - Healthcare workers
column10:
PHM- BORDER MEASURES:
 The four latent cases identified occurred among 300,000 travellers screened by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)-supported exit screening programmes in Guinea, Liberia and Sierra Leone, and all were asymptomatic (and hence undetectable) at the point of both exit screening and entry screening. Screening protocols included clear guidance on procedures for self-monitoring of symptoms [9], which ultimately led to rapid confirmation and isolation among the three latent cases in medical workers.

CLINICAL DATA: The study identified four latent Ebola cases exported during the 201416 epidemic, all initially asymptomatic at screening but later presenting with acute symptoms (fever, respiratory distress, weakness) that required hospitalization. These cases highlight the challenge of early detection, as three were healthcare workers who self-isolated upon symptom onset, while one traveler was misdiagnosed initially. In Europe, 17 confirmed Ebola cases were found since 1976 of whom, 16 occurred during the 2014-2016 Ebola disease epidemic and one during the 2026 Bundibugyo virus epidemic. In North America, 11 confirmed Ebola cases were identified since 1976 and all 11 cases occurred during the 2014-16 Ebola epidemic. Overall, of the 28 confirmed Ebola disease cases, five died. All three secondary cases were due to nosocomial infection of healthcare workers treating a known Ebola disease patient.

Epidemiological Parameter - RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION: Of the 28 confirmed cases identified, three secondary cases were due to nosocomial infection of healthcare workers treating a known Ebola disease patient. 

The vast majority of Ebola cases outside Africa (27 of 28) were either primary cases in individuals with occupational/exposure-related roles (e.g., healthcare workers, responders) or secondary cases in healthcare workers treating imported patients. Only one case occurred in a traveler without such exposure, which is concerning as it led to further transmission. However, the overall risk of exportation remains low and can be effectively reduced through infection control measures, travel screening, and monitoring of response workers, as aligned with WHO guidelines.

Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES FOR OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION:
 The authors calculated a crude risk estimate of 0.81 cases outside Africa, including medically evacuated, latent and secondary cases, per 1,000 Ebola disease cases in a source outbreak.  Focusing on latent cases and their secondary cases only, there have been 0.17 cases outside Africa per 1,000 Ebola disease cases in the source outbreak. 

For cases exported from the 201416 epidemic in Western Africa, we carried out a time-varying risk analysis using Poisson regression of the number of exported cases for a given epidemiological week and country. Over the course of the epidemic, the case exportation risk per reported source outbreak case decreased by 4.6% per week (95% CI: 1.77.4%; p?=?0.0097). More generally, the case exportation risk was substantially lower after the epidemic peak (0.46; 95% CI: 0.170.73 exported cases per 1,000 source cases from 27 October 2014 onwards) than it was before the peak (1.7; 95% CI: 0.952.7 exported cases per 1,000 source cases). This represents a 73% reduction in exportation risk per outbreak case between these two periods.
column11: Researchers used manual and AI-assisted internet searches to identify all laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases outside Africa, reviewing scientific articles, public health bulletins, and news reports to compile data from 27 studies, 9 bulletins, 82 news articles, and 9 other sources. The study included only confirmed cases of Bundibugyo, Ebola, and Sudan virus infections, excluding suspected cases, lab-acquired injuries, and Reston virus infections, to focus on epidemiologically relevant human transmissions.

column2: Bundibugyo virus outbreak: when a concerning pathogen meets a humanitarian emergency
Responding to Ebola: a test
Recommendations: Rapid Synthesis of Social and Behavioural Science learnings on Ebola for the Bundibugyo Virus Outbreak (2026) Ituri, DRC
column3: Other evidence synthesis, Other grey literature report
column6: This briefing outlines key considerations and recommendations drawn from a rapid synthesis of lessons learned from prior social and behavioural science (SBS) research highlighting that Ebola response in Ituri must prioritize trust-building by addressing historical grievances and avoiding coercive tactics, with tailored infection prevention, contact tracing, and border management that respect local social networks and languages. Additionally, emphasis is placed on effective community engagement, which requires partnering with trusted leaders, vulnerable groups, and high-risk occupations (e.g., boda boda drivers) while adapting safe burials and risk communication to cultural contexts and actionable, multilingual guidance.
column7: KAB and uptake; Quarantine - PHM; Border measure - PHM; Workflow guidance - IPC; Indirect -  lifestyle
column10:
Public Health Measures: Border communities between DRC and neighboring countries are deeply interconnected, with daily cross-border movement for essential activities like farming, water collection, market access, and healthcare which is driven by perceived better health services in Uganda. Past messaging restricting border crossings pushed people toward riskier, undetectable routes (panya routes), while boda boda taxi drivers and informal trade networks (including state-involved smuggling) continue to facilitate movement, even after Ugandas shutdown of lake border crossings, which persist informally via unregulated points. Community networks can improve early disease detection and access to care; past evidence shows cases were referred to an integrated treatment centre with EVD testing and isolation. 

Infection Prevention and Control (IPC) - General workflow guidance: IPC guidance needs to be  in community health facilities, where health worker fatalities have occurred, and extended to caregivers and high-risk groups like boda boda drivers and market vendors. In eastern DRC, heightened fear among these public-facing occupations requires actionable, tailored IPC measures that prioritize safety without stigmatization, addressing their unique risks while maintaining community trust.

Indirect impacts - Social (lifestyle/behavior changes): Past SBS research in eastern DRC on Ebola outbreaks has shown an atmosphere of mistrust in interactions between responders and communities, rooted in historical grievances like colonial violence, perceived humanitarian exploitation, and political manipulation (e.g., "Ebola as a business" during the 201820 outbreak). This mistrust is fueled by inconsistent resource allocation, contested authority, and unmet humanitarian needs during non-epidemic periods, making fear-based or context-blind responses likely to fail. However, SBS research demonstrates that localized, trust-building actions such as family-inclusive treatment centers, respectful burials, and media engagement with trusted community figures can humanize the response and foster cooperation
column11: The authors conducted a rapid synthesis including lessons learned from prior Ebola social and behavioural science (SBS) research and highlights critical insights for response efforts. It highlights critical insights for locally adapted and contextually informed response efforts that take account of the broader humanitarian crisis.

column1: Fanelli, et al, (2026), Journal of travel medicine
column2: Estimating importation risk of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease to Europe under different outbreak expansion scenarios
column3: Primary - short communication, Modeling, Peer-reviewed publication
column6: This study is a published version of a preprint and it estimated the relative importation risk of Bundibugyo to Europe  through air travel under six different outbreak expansion scenarios.  Potential spread across the Ugandan border produced no change compared to the refence scenario. Expansion across the border to South Sudan produced a first structural increase in importation pressure through the connectivity associated to Juba (5-fold increase for Northern Europe and 6-fold increase for Eastern Europe relative to localized transmission in DRC). Hypothetical amplification in Kampala, Kigali, and Kinshasa substantially increased importation pressure (>120 fold and 80 -fold) and reshaped exposure patterns across Europe.
column10:
Epidemiological Parameters - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE: 
Assessed relative exposure to Ebola virus disease importation into Europe under six outbreak expansion scenarios reflecting plausible pathways of geographical spread, including cross-border transmission and amplification in highly connected regional capitals. 


Potential spread across the Ugandan border produced no change in either the relative exposure of European countries or their overall importation pressure , compared with the reference scenario of established community transmission in Eastern DRC, with Italy, France, Belgium, and the UK being the most exposed. Expansion across the border to South Sudan produced a first structural increase in importation pressure through the connectivity associated to Juba (5-fold increase for Northern Europe and 6-fold increase for Eastern Europe relative to localized transmission in DRC), while still leaving UK, France, and Italy among the highest-risk countries. Further amplification in highly connected capitals in the region substantially altered importation patterns. Kampala and Kigali amplification increased exposure in Northern Europe, with Kampala producing a >120-fold increase in importation pressure relative to the reference scenario. Kinshasa amplification instead primarily reinforced exposure in Western Europe (~80-fold increase). In each case, the inclusion of the capital became the dominant source of importation pressure, reflecting its strong international connectivity.
column11: The study assessed Ebola importation risk to Europe using International Air Transport Association origindestination passenger flow data from 2024. Researchers modeled six outbreak expansion scenarios, including localized transmission in eastern DRC, cross-border spread to Uganda and South Sudan, and hypothetical amplification in major urban hubs (Kampala, Kigali, Kinshasa). The relative risk of importation for each European country was normalized to the total passenger flow from the source airports to Europe.

column1: Okingo, et al, (2026), Journal of travel medicine
column2: Estimating the introduction risk and sustained transmission of the Ebola Bundibugyo outbreak 2026 in the Democratic Republic of Congo
column3: Primary - short communication, Modeling, Peer-reviewed publication
column6: The authors estimated introduction risk and sustained transmission potential of the Bundibugyo virus outbreak in the Ituri province, Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). They found that introduction risk was highest in the health zones close to the reported  foci, specifically in Ituri, Nord-Kivu, and Haut-Uele. High sustained-transmission potential was distributed across DRC, including Kinshasa, Kasaÿ-Oriental, Kongo-Central, Tshopo, Nord-Kivu, Haut-Katanga, Sud-Kivu, Kasaÿ, and Kasaÿ-Central, suggesting preparedness in health zones with high sustained-transmission potential but lower introduction risk.
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration: 

The authors estimated the potential for an introduced case to generate sustained transmission in the DRC and found that introduction risk was highest in health zones (HZs) close to the reported foci and high sustained-transmission potential was distributed across DRC,  including Kinshasa, KasaÿOriental, Kongo-Central, Tshopo, Nord-Kivu, Haut-Katanga, Sud-Kivu, Kasaÿ, and Kasaÿ-Central.  Crossing the two rankings identified eight HZs that combined high introduction risk and high sustained-transmission potential: Binza Ozone and Binza Meteo in Kinshasa; Karisimbi in Nord-Kivu; Kadutu and Ibanda in Sud-Kivu; and Makiso-Kisangani, Kabondo, and Mangobo in Tshopo.
column11: The authors used multitype branching processes to estimate  for each DRC health zone (HZ), the potential for an introduced case to generate sustained transmission. They used  available population and mobility data to parameterize spatial transmission, testing a broad range of assumptions on disease transmissibility and on how local and between-community contact patterns can be inferred from these data. They then ranked HZs by sustained-transmission potential and compared them with introduction-risk ranks estimated from mobility data.

Bundibugyo Ebola in DR Congo and Uganda: community-centred response must be operational, not rhetorical
Development of an immunochromatographic lateral flow assay for the rapid detection of all known orthoebolavirus glycoproteins
column6: This study developed a prototype immunochromatographic lateral flow rapid diagnostic test (ILF -RDT ) that detects the glycoproteins (GPs) from all known orthoebolaviruses, including Bundibugyo virus (BDBV). The ILF-RDT detects both recombinant GP and authentic virus in samples spiked with human blood and experimentally infected animals and non-human primate samples at 7 days post-infection for BDBV, before animals exhibited severe disease. The ILF -RDT detects Ebola virus (EBOV), Sudan virus (SUDV), and BDBV GPs, both EBOV and BDBV GP produced positive results down to 31.2 ng, while SUDV GP was more difficult to interpret, with positive results at 500 and 250 ng but equivocal results at lower amounts of recombinant protein . The ILF -RDT was unable to detect GP from the more distantly related filovirus, Marburg virus, indicating that the test is specific for orthoebolaviruses but does not replace more sensitive diagnostic tests such as RT-qPCR.
column8: Human - General population, Animal - model of disease
column10:
Diagnostic test accuracy: 
Both EBOV and SUDV (secreted glycoprotein) sGP were detected in this assay, with SUDV sGP producing particularly robust positive results at every dilution tested. After evaluating the performance of the prototype ILF -RDT in the presence of human plasma or whole blood, the RDT detected EBOV GP, EBOV sGP, SUDV sGP, and BDBV GP at amounts as low as 62.5 or 31.2 ng. SUDV GP was clearly detected down to 250 ng, with equivocal results at lower amounts. To determine whether the test could detect authentic virus, SUDV and BDBV all produced clear positive results. The authors also evaluated the assay using whole blood samples obtained from experimentally infected ferrets and macaques and the EBOV-infected ferrets showed clear positive results at 5 days post -infection (DPI), corresponding to a Ct value of ~9, when the animals were severely ill while BDBV-infected ferrets were positive when they tested at 7 days post-infection DPI with CT values around 8 for BDBV. In macaques, 8 of 10 animals, remained positive for EBOV at 7 DPI (with Ct values ~19 -22), with two other animals (#3 and #7) showing very faint bands at the test line. 

While EBOV variants Makona -C07, Kikwit, and Mayinga all produced positive results, Makona -C05 did not. GP sequence analysis revealed only a single amino acid difference between Makona -C07 and Makona -C05, namely a tryptophan to arginine mutation at position 291 (W291R) (Fig. S4B). Importantly, this mutation is rare: analysis of all 2,985 available orthoebolavirus GP sequences in GenBank revealed that only 14 possessed the W291R substitution, with all 14 belonging exclusively to sequences of EBOV variant Makona -C05. A subsequent ELISA demonstrated that monoclonal antibodies (mAb) 5A3, unlike 6H8, did not bind to the GP from Makona -C05, suggesting that the W291R point mutation specifically obstructs binding to the assay s capture mAb.
column11: The researchers developed an immunochromatographic lateral flow assay (ILF-RDT) by generating orthoebolavirus-specific monoclonal antibodies, conjugating detection antibodies to gold nanoparticles, and spraying capture antibodies onto nitrocellulose membranes. Test strips were assembled with laminated pads, and samples (recombinant proteins, authentic viruses, and infected animal blood) were tested using a specialized running buffer. The assay was validated for specificity and sensitivity, with results confirmed via RT-qPCR and protein sequence analysis.

column1: NzanzuTJ, et al, (2026), Student's Journal of Health Research Africa
column2: Understanding the epidemiology of Ebola Bundibugyo virus (EBV) in selected populations: a cross-sectional study in Buhendera and Bwamba counties, Bundibugyo district
column6: In a cross-sectional study carried out in 6 sites, risk factors, distribution, endemicity and mode of action and transmission of the Ebola-Bundibugyo (EBV) strain after the 2007 outbreak were examined. This study found that EBV was influenced by gender roles (males more exposed through hunting/gathering), limited healthcare access, religious affiliation (Catholics most affected), occupation (farmers near reservoirs at higher risk), low income (increased hunting/gathering), larger family size, cultural practices (sleeping near/washing the dead), climatic conditions (harvest season increasing human-animal contact), and human movement patterns (with Bundibugyo as a central hub).
column7: Infection risk factors; Mortality; pathogen structural characterization
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION:
Gender: Males were at higher risk due to activities like hunting and gathering wild fruits (58.6% vs. 41.4% females; p < 0.05).

Healthcare Access: Limited access was significant, with 72.2% reporting relatives lacking medical care (p < 0.001).

Religion: Catholics experienced the highest cases (24.5%), followed by SDA (12.9%) and Muslims (17.7%; p < 0.05).

Occupation: Farming near animal reservoirs was critical, as the first outbreak occurred in a maize farmer (p < 0.01).

Income: Low income increased reliance on high-risk activities (p < 0.05).

Family Size: Larger family size showed a significant linear relationship with EBV occurrence (p < 0.001).

Tribe: Tribal differences had no significant impact (p > 0.05).

Climate: Bimodal rainfall and harvesting seasons heightened human-animal interaction (p < 0.05).

Structural characterization: EBV under an electron microscope is an anatomically  helical,  single-stranded,  unsegmented  RNA negative sense virus. Envelope glycoprotein is initially produced as a precursor known as pre-GP, cleaved by furin into two subunits, GP1 and GP2, which remain associated through a disulfide linkage between Cys53 of GP1 and Cys609 of GP2.
column11: This cross-section observational study in Bundibugyo District, Western Uganda, investigated Ebola Bundibugyo virus (EBV) using interviews with farmers, climatic assessments, and data from hospital and district health records. Snowball sampling identified households affected by EBV, while questionnaires and interview guides collected data on cultural risk factors (e.g., sleeping near/washing the dead, burial rituals). EBVs mode of action, structure, and biochemistry were analyzed using IgG ELISA on preserved samples at UVRI and CDC literature reviews.

column1: Wight, et al, (2026), Emerging infectious diseases
column2: Antibodies Cross-Reactive with Bundibugyo Virus in Ferrets Vaccinated with Ebola Virus Vaccine
column3: Primary - short communication, Experimental, Peer-reviewed publication
column6: To establish whether cross-reactive immune responses against  Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) can be elicited by heterologous recombinant vesicular stomatitis virus (rVSV)based vaccine, the authors sought to evaluate the levels of BDBV-specific IgG in the context of a ferret model. They found that the rVSV-EBOV vaccine elicits a moderate cross-reactive immune response against BDBV, while rVSV-SUDV does not,  suggesting rVSV-EBOV could potentially offer some protection against BDBV, though further research is needed to confirm.
column10:
Vaccine immunity - Measures of protective immunity (antibodies/titres/cytokines): 

Authors collected serum samples from ferrets 27 days after vaccination with rVSV-EBOV or rVSV-SUDV and quantified EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV glycoprotein-specific IgG levels by using ELISA. Both rVSV-EBOV and rVSV-SUDV elicited strong humoral responses to their homologous antigens; serum from all animals exhibited maximum absorbance at dilutions of 1:400.  At 16,582, the geometric mean endpoint titer for BDBV glycoprotein-specific IgG was significantly lower than that for EBOV glycoprotein (Wilcoxon statistical test = 3; p = 1.6 æ 104), despite limited overlap in the range of titers, which extended from 3,200 to 30,270. We detected no EBOV- or BDBV-specific IgG in the control animals. 

The animals survived subsequent challenge with a uniformly lethal dose of the corresponding virus. Serum samples from ferrets vaccinated with rVSV-EBOV exhibited moderate levels of absorbance against BDBV glycoprotein, in contrast with serum samples from animals vaccinated with rVSV-SUDV, which exhibited very low levels of absorbance.  rVSV-EBOV vaccination elicits BDBV-specific antibodies, extending observations that have already been made in humans.
column11: Animal studies were conducted at the Canadian Science Centre for Human and Animal Health (CSCHAH) under protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care Committee, adhering to Canadian Council on Animal Care guidelines. Ferrets were vaccinated intramuscularly with rVSV-EBOV or rVSV-SUDV (2 æ 10? PFU/dose) or saline (controls), with serum collected pre-vaccination and on day 27 post-vaccination. IgG levels specific to EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV glycoproteins were measured using indirect ELISA, and statistical analysis (Wilcoxon test) compared reciprocal endpoint titers for EBOV and BDBV glycoproteins.
