column1: Akilimali, et al, (2026), The New England journal of medicine
column2: Clinical Characteristics of Patients Infected with Bundibugyo Virus, DRC 2026
column3: Primary - short communication, Observational, Peer-reviewed publication
column6: This retrospective cohort study analyzed 2,351 recorded cases of suspected  Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD), finding 505 laboratory-confirmed cases (21.5%) and 635 negative tests (27.0%), with confirmed cases showing higher mortality than those that tested negative (18.8% vs. 10.2%).  Demographic and symptom profiles were similar across groups, with confirmed cases more likely to present with fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, and systemic signs than those that tested negative.  Among 129 confirmed cases , a 7.4-day mean delay between symptom onset and sample collection was observed and among 253 confirmed cases, patients who died had lower cycle-threshold (Ct) values, indicating a higher viral load, than those with no recorded death (median Ct value, 24.4 vs. 28.7; mean difference in Ct value, ?3.7.
column7: Other Epi; Acute infection characteristics; Other clinical data
column10:
CLINICAL DATA Acute infection: 
Patients with confirmed BVD frequently presented with fever (74.3%), gastrointestinal symptoms (vomiting 66.7%, diarrhea 67.4%), and systemic signs like headache (42.2%) and difficulty breathing (34.1%), while hemorrhagic signs were rare (10.4%).  

In confirmed cases, the symptom profile varied little according to patient sex or age after mutual adjustment. Fever was somewhat less frequent in male patients than in female patients (adjusted risk ratio, 0.88; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.78 to 1.00), and headache became less frequent with increasing age (adjusted risk ratio, 0.89 per 10-year increment; 95% CI, 0.83 to 0.96). Cough was modestly more common in male patients than in female patients (adjusted risk ratio, 1.89; 95% CI, 1.01 to 3.53), as was chest pain (adjusted risk ratio, 1.94; 95% CI, 1.01 to 3.71), whereas the relative risk of other signs and symptoms did not vary substantially according to patient sex or age. 

The signs-and-symptoms profile distinguished confirmed BVD cases from negative cases, with confirmed patients more likely to report vomiting (66.7% vs. 50.8%), diarrhea (67.4% vs. 49.6%), anorexia (39.5% vs. 17.2%), abdominal pain (37.3% vs. 16.3%), difficulty breathing (34.1% vs. 10.9%), and difficulty swallowing (32.8% vs. 10.7%); fever was also more frequent in confirmed cases (74.3% vs. 64.9%), while hemorrhagic signs showed no substantial difference (10.4% vs. 10.3%).

CLINICAL DATA OTHER:
Patients with confirmed BVD were demographically similar to those who tested negative; in both groups, the majority of patients were adults 20 and 39 years of age, and there were slightly more female patients than male patients.

RISK FACTORS OF MORTALITY :
Among 253 patients with laboratory-confirmed positive samples tested with the RADIONE PCR assay2 from the laboratory in Bunia, Ituri Province, DRC (as of June 5, 2026), patients who died had lower cycle-threshold (Ct) values, indicating a higher viral load, than those with no recorded death (median Ct value, 24.4 [interquartile range, 21.3 to 26.9] vs. 28.7 [interquartile range, 25.0 to 32.5]; mean difference in Ct value, ?3.7 [95% CI, ?5.2 to ?2.3]).

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL PARAMETER - OTHER:
Among 129 patients who tested positive for whom the symptom-onset and sample dates were recorded, the mean delay, with accounting for right truncation and censoring was 7.4 days (95% credible interval, 5.3 to 13.5; median, 4.8 days [95% credible interval, 3.5 to 7.8]). The 7.4-day mean delay between symptom onset and the date on which samples were obtained may reflect a lack of diagnostic capacity early during the outbreak.
column11:
We report here signs and symptoms and basic laboratory data from the current 2026 BVD outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). 

Among 2351 recorded cases in the individual-level database for BVD between May 3 and June 8, 2026, a total of 505 patients (21.5%) had a laboratory-confirmed case of BVD (as determined by Altona Diagnostics and RADIONE polymerase-chain-reaction [PCR] tests2), and 635 patients (27.0%) with suspected BVD tested negative. The remaining patients were untested, had pending results, or had results that remained classified as being suspected or probable. Among the 505 patients with laboratory-confirmed BVD who had records in the database, 95 (18.8%) had died by the data-cutoff date, as compared with 65 of the 635 patients (10.2%) who had tested negative. This finding cannot be interpreted as the case fatality rate owing to limited data on final outcome and unknown denominators.

column1: Sullivan, et al, (2026), The New England journal of medicine
column2: Bundibugyo Virus Disease in 2026 - Clinical and Public Health Responses
Projected epidemiologic and economic impact of the 7-1-7 outbreak response framework in Uganda: a stochastic modelling study of Bundibugyo Ebola virus
column6: This study uses a counterfactual scenario modelling approach to answer: "what would be the projected epidemiologic and economic impact if Uganda implements a rapid 7-1-7 (detection ?7 days, notification ?1 day, response ?7 days) compared with a delayed response, under varying importation pressures?" by using a rapid 3-1-5 response (detection 3 days, notification 1 day, response 5 days) against a delayed counterfactual (detection 11 days, notification 10 days,response 12 days),. They found that a rapid 3-1-5 response (detection 3 days, notification 1 day, response 5 days) reduces median cumulative cases by 6066% and deaths by 6263% compared to a delayed counterfactual (11-10-12 days), across import probabilities from 10% to the observed 56%; the rapid response is strictly dominant (cost-saving and life-saving) for all import levels, with total costs of USD 29.129.9 million versus USD 37.438.1 million for the delayed scenario; the case fatality rate, import probability, and basic reproduction number are the dominant drivers of outcome uncertainty.
column7: Size or Duration estimate; Mortality; Economic cost of disease
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION:

Rapid response: Median cumulative cases ranged from 26-31 with a narrow interquartile range (21-39).  Under the rapid 3-1-5 response, cumulative infections rise slowly and plateau below 40 cases by day 90, with overlapping uncertainty bands across import levels. 

Delayed response: the delayed scenario produced substantially larger outbreaks: median cases ranged from 76 (10% import) to 80 (30% import) and 78 (56% import), with upper 95% uncertainty limits exceeding 120 cases.  

CLINICAL DATA: MORTALITY pg15
Rapid response:   Median deaths were 3 across all import levels, and health worker infections ranged from 6 to 8. 

Delayed response: Median deaths were 8 for all delayed scenarios, health worker infections were 2731, 

ECONOMIC COSTS

Rapid response:   Total costs were approximately USD 29.129.9 million, and DALYs were 101 for all import levels. 

Delayed scenario:  Total costs were USD 37.438.1 million. 

DALYs in the delayed scenario were 270285, approximately 2.7 times higher than in the rapid response. The rapid 3-1-5 scenario therefore averted 5254 deaths per 100,000 population compared to the delayed response, with cost savings of USD 7.58.6 million per import level. The incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) was negative for all comparisons, confirming that the rapid response is strictly dominant (less costly and more effective)
column11:
This study uses a stochastic SEIR model to evaluate the *7-1-7  ( (detection ?7 days, notification ?1 day, response ?7 days) outbreak response framework in Uganda with the Bundibugyo Ebola strain  Using a stochastic SEIR model parameterised for Uganda with the Bundibugyo Ebola strain and three daily importation probabilities (10%, 30%, and the observed 56%), we compared a rapid 3-1-5 response (detection 3 days, notification 1 day, response 5 days) against a delayed counterfactual (detection 11 days, notification 10 days, response 12 days).  The Sobol global sensitivity analysis was used to quantify the contribution of 16 parameters to the uncertainty in total deaths. Variance-based Sobol sensitivity analysis identified the case fatality rate, import probability, and basic reproduction number as the most influential parameters, with detection and response delays contributing through interactions.


All parameters (Table 1) were obtained from the Uganda Ministry of Health Situation Report No. 005 (26 May 2026) (MOH Uganda, 2026) and the updated Situation Report of 8 June 2026 (MOH, 8 June 2026), the WHO risk assessment (WHO PHEIC, 2026), a meta-analysis of Bundibugyo strain 0 (Muzembo et al., 2024), and literature on Ebola transmission dynamics (Althaus, 2014; Velasquez et al., 2015). The 0 was set to 2.76  the upper bound of the 95% confidence interval (1.252.76) reported for the Bundibugyo strain  to reflect the most transmissible scenario and provide a conservative test of the rapid response. 


Costs were estimated from the health system perspective in 2026 USD. The Uganda Ministry of Healths 4W matrix (Who is doing What, Where, and When) provided a detailed 90-day fixed response budget of USD 24,707,459, disaggregated into 11 operational pillars. Because most of these costs are fixed regardless of outbreak size (e.g., maintaining the Emergency Operations Centre, laboratories, and coordination structures), we derived a cost per case from the median case count under the rapid 7-1-7 scenario with the lowest importation risk (10%). The median number of cases in that scenario was 3, giving a cost per case of USD 24,707,459 / 3 ? USD 8,235,820. For other scenarios, total cost = cost per case æ median cases. Disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) were calculated as DALY = YLL + YLD. Years of life lost (YLL) = deaths æ (life expectancy at age of death ? age at death), using average age at death = 35 years and life expectancy at birth for Uganda = 68 years (WHO 2023).   Years lived with disability (YLD) = cases æ disability weight æ duration, with disability weight for acute EVD = 0.133 and duration of acute disability = 180 days (0.493 years) (GBD 2021 Disability Weights Collaborators, 2024). The incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) compared the delayed scenario (baseline) to the rapid 7-1-7 scenario (intervention) for each import level.

column2: Lethal VSV-based surrogate mouse models for Sudan and Bundibugyo ebolaviruses enable antibody evaluation under BSL-2 conditions
column6: This study generated recombinant vesicular stomatitis viruses expressing SUDV or BDBV glycoproteins and  established  lethal  infection  models  in  Type  I interferon receptor knockout mice.  Bot viruses induced rapid, dose-dependent mortality; infected mice developed high viral loads in the liver,  spleen, and  lungs,  along with leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated  transaminases  indicating systemic infection and liver injury. Treatment with a single monoclonal  antibody  rEBOV-515   completely protected mice from lethal challenge, reducing viral loads and  restoring  body  weight, establishing reproducible surrogate models for studying  SUDV and BDBV  infection and provide an accessible experimental platform for evaluating GP-targeting therapeutic strategies.
column10:
Infection immunity -  MEASURES OF PROTECTIVE antibodies/titres/cytokines):

 In  the  VSV?G-SUDV  GP  cohort,  untreated  mice  exhibited rapid  and  sustained  weight  loss,  succumbing  by  day  4  post infection.  In  contrast,  antibody-treated  animals  showed transient  weight  reduction  followed  by  full  recovery,  resulting in 100% survival . Similarly, infection with VSV?G-BDBV GP induced progressive weight loss beginning at day 2, with  complete  mortality  by  day  7  in  untreated  controls. Administration  of  mAb  515  markedly  attenuated  weight  loss and  conferred  complete  protection,  with  all  treated  mice surviving to the study endpoint. Quantitative  analysis  of  viral  burden  in  major  organs revealed  that  antibody  treatment  prevented  an  increase  in both viral RNA copies and infectious titers. In VSV?G-SUDV GP  infected  mice,  mAb  515  reduced  viral  RNA  loads  by approximately 104.4 copies/g in the liver and 104.2 copies/g in the  lungs,  while  decreasing  live  virus  titers  by  roughly  103.4 TCID50 /g in the liver. Similarly, in VSV?G-BDBV GP infected mice, treatment led to reductions of 103.8 copies/g in the liver and  102.8 copies/g  in  the  spleen,  with  corresponding decreases  of  101.6 TCID50 /g  in  liver  tissue  but  no  significant differences were observed in the brain tissue. These results indicate that mAb 515 prevents or significantly restricts  viral  dissemination  in  target  organs,  effectively curbing viral dissemination and disease progression in IFNAR- / - mice.
column11: This study used Vero E6 and BSR-T7 cells cultured in DMEM with FBS and antibiotics, and 293F cells in SMM 293-TII medium. A broadly neutralizing antibody, mAb 515, targeting a conserved Ebola virus glycoprotein (GP) epitope, was expressed in 293F cells, purified, and characterized. Recombinant vesicular stomatitis viruses (VSVs) expressing Sudan virus (SUDV) or Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) GP genes were rescued in BSR-T7 cells. Viral growth kinetics were assessed in Vero E6 cells at an MOI of 0.01, with titers quantified using TCID50 assays. Animal studies in IFNAR-/- mice (lacking type I interferon receptors) evaluated viral pathogenicity, antibody efficacy, and systemic infection. LD50 values were determined, and tissues were analyzed for viral load via RT-qPCR and TCID50. Histopathology was performed using H&E and IHC staining, following INHAND guidelines. Statistical analysis used SEM and appropriate tests (t-test, ANOVA).

Size of the 2026 Ebola outbreak and risk of cross-border spillover from Bundibugyo virus in Ituri Province, DR Congo, and its implications for preparedness: a recalibrated stochastic modelling study
column6: This study aimed to establish a short-term trajectory of the BDBV outbreak and probability of cross-border spillover into countries with elevated risk of importation to guide preparedness priorities. Aligning with the central scenario (median R? = 1.71), the current outbreak is projected to have 990 cumulative confirmed cases and 174 deaths by Week 12, and cross-border spillover risks are highest for Uganda (94.2% importation probability) and South Sudan (69.3%) , requiring urgent preparedness.
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration:
Central transmissibility scenario (mean accepted R0=1ñ71): The ensemble projected a median of 648 cumulative confirmed cases by week 10 (June 8, 2026; 50% prediction interval [PI] 560732), rising to 990 cases by week 12 (June 24, 2026: 50% PI 8581141 and 90% PI 7091293), with a projected median of 174 deaths at week 12. 

Low transmissibility scenario  (mean accepted R0=1ñ42): Cumulative confirmed cases are projected to reach a median of 870 by week 12 (50% PI 7521004 and 90% PI 6411133; 160 deaths) and 3662 by week 24 (50% PI 28844484 and 90% PI 20946037) with 673 deaths

High transmissibility scenario (mean accepted R0=2ñ08): confirmed cases projected to reach a median of 1364 (50% PI 11541579 and 90% PI 9751807; 202 deaths) by week 12, rising to 66?420 (50% PI 56?56078?937 and 90% PI 41?71895?881; 10?644 deaths) by week 24. The observed confirmed-case trajectory is consistent with the central scenario. 

The model predicts very high cross-border spillover risk for Uganda (94.2% importation probability, 19 confirmed cases as of June 4) and elevated risk for South Sudan (69.3%, driven by porous borders and sustained transmission in DR Congo), while Rwanda and Burundi face low risk (8.6% and 2.0%, respectively). Ugandas outbreak is already established with local transmission, and South Sudans risk remains substantial despite DR Congos trajectory aligning with lower scenarios.
column11:
The study used a stochastic SEIRD model (susceptible, exposed, infectious, recovered, dead) calibrated to laboratory-confirmed Bundibugyo virus cases in DR Congo (598 cases as of June 8, 2026) to project outbreak trajectories under low, central, and high transmissibility scenarios. A linked daily-hazard spillover model estimated cross-border importation risks for Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, and Burundi over 12 weeks, incorporating mobility, detection sensitivity, and travel-while-infectious parameters. Sensitivity analyses tested assumptions for travel, reporting completeness, and catchment size.

Case data were taken from the WHO AFRO External Situation Reports and the WHO AFRO internal tracker through to June 8, 2026. The laboratory-confirmed case series was used as the primary calibration metric: confirmed cases rose from 33 on May 18, 2026, to 105 on May 24, 2026, and 598 on June 8, 2026.15 Laboratory-confirmed cases were preferred over suspected cases because they are specific to BDBV and not subject to the over-ascertainment affecting suspected counts.

column2: Can stochastic modelling predict cross-border spread of the Bundibugyo Ebola virus outbreak in Africa?
Network-based modelling of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease importation and spread in Uganda using Displacement Tracking Matrix flow data and non-pharmaceutical intervention compliance scenarios
column6: This study assesses whether costly nationwide non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) are needed when the 7-1-7 agile response (detection within 3 days, notification within 1 day, response within 5 days) already includes a strong behavioural component. Mobility data from the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) showed Kisoro had the highest import (in-strength 3,823) and export (out-strength 1,350) risk, while Kampala had substantial import risk (in-strength 1,290) but lower export risk (out-strength 150); under baseline mobility, the model projected a median of 69 to 70 cumulative cases (95% CrI: 57 to 98) and 3 deaths over 90 days, with NPIs (community contact reduction, healthcare protection, movement restriction) at 20%, 40%, and 60% compliance showing no statistically significant case reduction.
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION: 

Under baseline mobility, the model projected a median of 6970 cumulative cases (95% CrI: 5798) , 11 367 HCW infections, over 90 days. Non-pharmaceutical interventions (community contact reduction, healthcare protection, movement restriction) at 20%, 40%, and 60% compliance produced no statistically significant reduction in cases. Superspreading events occurred in 34.640.6% of simulations. Kampala bore the highest predicted burden (median 22 cases, 100% outbreak probability more than 10 cases), followed by Wakiso (11 cases, 64.9%). Border districts had lower burdens (Bundibugyo 5, Kasese 3, Kisoro 2).

Sobol sensitivity analysis (500 samples, 200 bootstraps) identified the infectious period (first-order index 0.838), case fatality rate (0.738), and basic reproduction number (0.664) as the most influential parameters; mobility-related parameters had lower total-order indices.

MORTALITY:
Under baseline mobility, the model projected 3 deaths and 1 HCW deaths, over 90 days.
column11:
The authors constructed a data-driven directed weighted mobility network using the International Organization for Migration Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) flows collected from 1524 May 2026 (11,245 observed movements) and the 2024 Uganda census (45.9 million people). A stochastic metapopulation SEIR model, incorporating pre-symptomatic transmission and the movement of both exposed and infectious individuals, was simulated over 90 days across 135 Ugandan districts and two DRC provinces. Authors simulated three compliance levels (20%, 40%, 60%) for NPIs (risk communication, standard operating procedures, avoidance of handshaking, mass gathering bans) and assessed their impact on total cases, deaths, and healthcare worker (HCW) infections. A global sensitivity analysis using the Sobol method was used to identify which parameters most influence the total case count, thereby informing decision-makers on where to focus data collection and intervention efforts.

The median total cases ranged from 69 to 70 across compliance scenarios, with 3 deaths, 11 healthcare worker (HCW) infections, and 1 HCW death. The 95% credible intervals overlapped substantially (5898 cases for 20% compliance, 5795 for 60% compliance), indicating no statistically significant difference between compliance levels. The extinction probability was 0% across all scenarios, reflecting that the outbreak was already established at the start of the simulation (16 observed cases as of June 5, 2026). The probability of a large outbreak (?50 cases) was 100% in all scenarios, consistent with the projected median of approximately 70 cases. Superspreading events (?5 secondary infections from a single source in one day) occurred in 40.6% of simulations at 20% compliance, decreasing to 34.8% at 40% compliance and 34.6% at 60% compliance. The mean number of superspreading events per simulation ranged from 0.396 to 0.472, with a maximum of 3 in any single simulation.

column2: Longitudinal Bundibugyo Virus Glycoprotein Seroreactivity Following rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP Vaccination in Outbreak-Affected Populations of the Democratic Republic of the Congo
column6: This study collected serum samples between 2018-2023 from vaccine recipients in Mbandaka, Equateur Province and Beni, North Kivu Province and evaluated development of antibodies to Bundibugyo glycoprotein (GP) following rVSV?GZEBOV-GP vaccination in two EVD outbreak-affected populations. They found that baseline Bundibugyo virus disease (BDBV) GP seroreactivity differed between sites, with 3.3% of participants reactive in Mbandaka and 10.4% in Beni.  In Mbandaka, BDBV GP titers remained unchanged through 6 months post-vaccination but increased markedly between 2.5 and 3.5 years, accompanied by a rise in seroreactivity to 35.3%, followed by waning at later visits. In Beni, BDBV GP titers increased rapidly after vaccination, reaching peak seroreactivity at 21 days (52.3%) and 6 months (53.9%), with mean fold increases of 21.9 and 20.1 among baseline naÿve participants.
column10:
Epidemiological Parameter - SEROPREVALENCE: 
Mbandaka cohort: At baseline, 3.3% (n=16) of participants were observed to be seroreactive to BDBV GP and 28.8% (n=139) of participants were reactive to EBOV GP. No change was observed in anti-BDBV GP antibody MFI values at 21 days or 6 months postvaccination. However, there was a rise in mean BDBV GP reactivity from 1238 to 4845 MFI (p<.0001) between the year 2.5 and year 3.5 visits. Additionally, at the 3.5 year visit, 35.3% (n=116) of participants were seroreactive based on the cut-off criteria. 

Beni cohort: At baseline. reactivity was observed to be 10.4% (n=62) to BDBV GP and 3.8% (n=23) to EBOV GP. There was an immediate rise in anti-BDBV GP antibody MFI values at 21 days and 6 months after vaccination with rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP from a mean of 1653 MFI to 6678 (p<.0001). At 21 days and 6 months post-vaccination the authors observed 52.3% (n=283) and 53.9% (n=270) seroreactivity to BDBV GP, respectively. Among those seronegative to BDBV GP at baseline and seroreactive at 21 days (n=241, 40.2%) and 6 months (n= 227, 37.9%), authors recorded a mean fold change of 21.9 and 20.1 compared to baseline, respectively. Mean antibody MFI values decreased for all antigens during the 6 month to 2.5 year postvaccination period, with only those for EBOV and BDBV GP remaining above the mean MFI values at baseline.
column11:
Between 2018 and 2023, serum samples were collected from vaccine recipients in Mbandaka, Equateur Province (n=482 at baseline), and Beni, North Kivu Province (n=599 at baseline). Antibody reactivity was assessed using a multiplex pan-filovirus immunoassay. We evaluated longitudinal trends in BDBV GP seroreactivity across follow-up visits extending to approximately five years after vaccination.  


Recruitment and enrollment of this study coincided with the rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP vaccination efforts undertaken in response to the DRCs 9th and 10th EVD outbreaks. Beginning June 2018 in Mbandaka, Equateur, and in August, 2018 in Beni, North Kivu, individuals ?1 year of age who received the rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP vaccine under the World Health Organization (WHO) ring vaccination protocol were eligible for enrollment

Development of Peptide Entry Inhibitors Targeting the Endosomal Receptor NPC1 Binding Site of Orthoebolavirus
column6: This study designed peptide inhibitors effective against BDBV based on the NiemannPick C1 (NPC1) receptor to target the NPC1 binding site to block viral entry. The cyclized peptide erp1c demonstrated strong in vitro inhibition of BDBV infection (IC?? = 13.73 çM) and partial in vivo protection in mice (25% survival), while its linear counterpart erp1 was less effective (IC?? = 17.89 çM).
column7: Infection immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Infection immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); pathogen  Protein-inhibitor/binding interactions
column10:
Protein-inhibitor/binding interactions:  

Both peptides erp1 and erp1c inhibited BDBV infection with IC50 values of 17.89 çM and 13.73 çM, respectively. Based on the side-by-side assay, peptide erp1c exhibited strong activity only against EBOV and BDBV with IC50 values of 11.6 çM and 11.3 çM, respectively, but no activity was observed against  Amphotropic murine leukemia virus,  suggesting that the inhibition from the peptide erp1c is specific to Orthoebolavirus or filoviruses and not others, because this peptide inhibitor was designed by specifically targeting the NPC1 binding site. The cyclized peptide erp1c (200 çg, twice daily for 7 days) provided partial protection in mice, with 2/8 surviving (25% survival) compared to 0/8 in untreated controls, indicating potential antiviral efficacy against MA EBOV.

After mutating two phenylalanines (FF) into two alanines (AA) in both forms of peptides erp1 (linear) and erp1c (cyclized) for functional analysis, the data displayed that in both linear and cyclized peptides show the mutated FF/AA peptides decreased the inhibition in a dose-dependent manner, compared with the counterpart wildtype peptide. The linear mutant peptide erp1aa increased the IC50 values from 25 çM to 109 çM with an estimated IC50 fold-change of 4.3; the cyclized mutant peptide erp1caa increased the IC50 values from 12.6 çM to 38.5 çM with an estimated IC50 fold-change of 3.0. These observed shifts in the inhibition curves strongly indicated that these two hydrophobic residues contribute significantly to the binding to the hydrophobic receptor binding sites. 

Molecular docking of cyclic peptides erp1c (wildtype) and erp1caa (FF/AA mutant) showed F7/F8 docked into NPC1s receptor-binding site, mimicking loop 2. Surprisingly, the mutant shifted V9/Y10 to the binding pocket instead of A7/A8 but had lower affinity, confirming F7/F8s critical role in viral entry inhibition.
column11: Peptides were synthesized (?95% purity) and viral glycoproteins (EBOV, BDBV) cloned into pCDNA3.1+ for pseudovirus production in 293T cells via PEI transfection. Inhibition assays included TZM-bl cell-based Luciferase tests (IC?? calculation) and HeLa cell immunostaining (infection efficiency via CellProfiler). In vivo studies comprised pharmacokinetic analysis (IV injection, LC-MS/MS) and efficacy testing (subcutaneous peptide treatment post-MA EBOV challenge), with survival, histopathology, and body weight monitored for 21 days under BSL-4 containment.
